current_fact.fact_text: On 13 April 1975, gunmen associated with the Phalangist militia attacked a bus in the Ain El Remmaneh district of Beirut, killing 26 Palestinians and sparking cycles of revenge. The "Beirut bus massacre" is widely seen as the flashpoint that ignited Lebanon’s 15-year civil war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

current_fact.match_reason: A single bus journey that helped plunge a country into war contrasts with an episode about travel as a gentle, perspective-shifting practice, hinting at how movement can either divide or heal.

queue.fact_text: On 13 April 1996, an Israeli helicopter fired rockets at an ambulance in the southern Lebanese village of Mansouri, killing two women and four children seeking refuge inside. The Mansouri attack became a stark symbol of how modern warfare often blurs the line between combatant and civilian, battlefield and home.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

queue.match_reason: A devastating strike on civilians in a conflict zone is paired with a roots-and-dub episode steeped in anti-war, "Babylon"-questioning traditions, offering a contemplative, critical lens on violence and power.

queue.fact_text: On 13 April 1997, a 21-year-old Tiger Woods won the Masters Tournament by a record 12 strokes, becoming the youngest golfer ever to wear the iconic green jacket. His victory at Augusta didn’t just shatter scoring records; it transformed golf’s global image and inspired a new generation of players from far beyond the sport’s traditional demographics.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: Tiger Woods’ record-breaking win capped years of obsessive practice and learning under pressure, fitting an episode that dissects how persistence, repetition, and delayed rewards fuel mastery.

queue.fact_text: On 13 April 2023, FBI agents arrested Air National Guardsman Jack Teixeira at his Massachusetts home after he allegedly leaked a trove of classified Pentagon documents in an online chat group. The case highlighted how a young, low-ranking service member armed with a computer and a printer could shake global diplomacy and intelligence sharing.

episode.title: Morning Drops #53 - Tyler, The Creator
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin follows the evolution of Tyler, the Creator, from the kid nobody took seriously to one of the most important artists of his generation. From a cockroach eaten on camera as a joke to Grammy stages shared with his mother, this episode traces a career built on stubbornness, reinvention, and the quiet courage of refusing to stay in one place.

Listen, move, and let no one put you in a box.

Tracklist:
Tyler, the Creator – Like Him (feat. Lola Young)
Tyler, the Creator – Yonkers
Tyler, the Creator – See You Again (feat. Kali Uchis)
Tyler, the Creator – Earfquake
Tyler, the Creator – Ring Ring Ring

queue.match_reason: A young, online-native figure suddenly thrust into global scrutiny mirrors an episode about a once-dismissed youth who grew into a world-shaping voice, highlighting generational power and digital-age visibility.

queue.fact_text: On 13 April 2024, a lone attacker went on a stabbing rampage through the Westfield Bondi Junction shopping centre in Sydney, killing six people and injuring twelve before being shot by police. The attack shattered a weekend afternoon in a crowded mall, reminding Australians how quickly ordinary public spaces can become scenes of horror.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A sudden act of violence in an everyday space connects with an episode about bodily vulnerability, recovery, and learning to move again after trauma, offering a gentle frame for processing shock.

queue.fact_text: On 13 April 1996 in sports-mad America, the same weekend Tiger Woods rewrote golf history, millions of casual fans also discovered how young excellence can redraw a sport’s future. His green jacket didn’t just mark a win; it marked the moment a new kind of athletic celebrity—media-savvy, global, and intensely branded—became the norm.

episode.title: Morning Drops #30 - Star Treatment
episode.description: A celebration of change and transformation. Gaia traces the evolution of Arctic Monkeys - from raw energy to cinematic sound - reflecting on how artists, and all of us, grow and change through time. A journey through courage, curiosity, and the beauty of slowing down.
songs: Arctic Monkeys - There’d Better Be a Mirrorball

queue.match_reason: A sporting icon’s rise into global stardom pairs with an episode literally titled "Star Treatment," both tracing how talent evolves into carefully crafted, transformative celebrity.

queue.fact_text: On 13 April 1960, when Transit 1-B began beeping its precise radio signals, it quietly inaugurated the era of satellite-guided navigation that would one day make it almost impossible to get lost. Early users needed slide rules and bulky receivers, but the principle behind that orbiting beacon now guides everything from airlines to rideshare apps.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: A pioneering navigation signal guiding vessels and submarines mirrors an evening mix that slowly guides listeners from medina alleys to wide-open dance landscapes, like a musical GPS across cultures.

queue.fact_text: On 13 April 1970, as Apollo 13’s crippled spacecraft coasted through space, NASA engineers on Earth had to improvise with exactly the hardware already aboard—famously using plastic bags, hoses, and tape to adapt carbon dioxide scrubbers. The "mailbox" fix turned a looming suffocation crisis into a triumph of creative problem-solving under pressure.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: A life-or-death hack built from imperfect materials aligns with an episode themed around wabi-sabi, celebrating improvisation, flaws, and making beauty out of what’s already there.

queue.fact_text: On 13 April 1970, when astronaut Jack Swigert reported "Houston, we've had a problem," the phrase instantly entered pop culture, but the real drama was the crew’s calm, methodical response to cascading failures. Their ability to adapt, trust checklists, and lean on ground teams turned a doomed Moon mission into one of humanity’s most studied case studies in crisis management.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A "bomb" of an oxygen tank that launched a new era of space safety pairs symbolically with Sedna FM’s own explosive first episode, both marking intense beginnings that defined everything that followed.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1970, an oxygen tank in the Apollo 13 service module exploded nearly 320,000 kilometers from Earth, crippling the spacecraft and turning a Moon landing into a fight for survival. Against staggering odds, NASA engineers and the crew improvised a lifeboat strategy using the lunar module, bringing all three astronauts safely home and turning disaster into one of spaceflight’s most iconic rescues.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A near-fatal space mission that required radical mindset shifts pairs with an episode about reimagining emptiness and turning it into possibility, powered by energetic, forward-moving tracks.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1960, the United States launched Transit 1-B, an experimental satellite that became the world’s first operational navigation satellite. Originally created to guide submarines carrying ballistic missiles, its Doppler-based system laid the technical foundations for the GPS constellation now quietly guiding phones, planes, and ships all over Earth.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: A pioneering navigation satellite that helped humans find their way fits with a sound-journey themed episode about destinations and voyages across musical territories.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1742, George Frideric Handel premiered his oratorio "Messiah" in Dublin rather than London, partly to test the work outside the capital’s critical glare. He packed the hall by asking ladies to forego hoops and gentlemen their swords to make room, and the performance was such a triumph that it helped revive his career and cemented "Messiah" as one of history’s most enduring choral works.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A landmark in long-form, uplifting sacred music resonates with a reflective, extended-jam jazz episode that also creates a meditative, almost liturgical atmosphere through sound.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1612, legendary swordsman Miyamoto Musashi arrived purposely late to his duel with Sasaki Kojirō on Ganryū-jima, carving a wooden sword from an oar on the way. He defeated Kojirō with that improvised weapon, and the encounter became one of Japan’s most storied duels, shaping the myth of Musashi as both strategist and unconventional artist of combat.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: An iconic duel defined by discipline, timing, and body control aligns with an episode rooted in combat sports training, physical practice, and the rhythm of movement.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1204, Crusader armies breached the walls of Constantinople and sacked the Byzantine capital, looting centuries of art, relics, and knowledge. The city’s fall shattered the empire and reshaped the map of the eastern Mediterranean, echoing through politics, religion, and culture for generations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A pivotal event in Mediterranean history is paired with a seaborne musical journey around the same sea, echoing the region’s entangled cultures and turbulent past.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1699, Guru Gobind Singh formalised the Khalsa, transforming the Sikh community into a brotherhood of "Warrior-Saints" bound by shared code, symbols, and identity. Initiates drank sweetened water stirred with a double-edged sword, symbolising the fusion of spiritual devotion with the duty to protect justice.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: The birth of a spiritually grounded warrior community resonates with Tuareg desert music shaped by faith, resistance, and communal identity in harsh landscapes.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1964, Sidney Poitier became the first Black man to win the Academy Award for Best Actor, honored for his role in "Lilies of the Field." His quiet, dignified performance and barrier-breaking win challenged Hollywood’s racial boundaries and opened doors for future generations of actors of color.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: A historic moment in representation and cinematic history pairs with an episode celebrating boundary-breaking artists, all about visibility, voice, and shifting who gets to be centre stage.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1943, Nazi Germany announced the discovery of mass graves of Polish officers in the Katyn Forest, revealing a massacre carried out by Soviet secret police in 1940. For decades the Soviet Union denied responsibility, and the truth about the atrocity became a bitter fault line in wartime diplomacy and Cold War memory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: A buried atrocity and contested memory connects with a moody, cinematic trip-hop set that feels like uncovering layers and confronting shadows beneath the surface.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1777, during the American Revolutionary War, Continental forces at Bound Brook, New Jersey were surprised at dawn by a British and Hessian attack. The small garrison was forced into a hasty retreat, and the clash became a study in the perils of overextension and the constant chess game of manoeuvre in a war for independence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A Revolutionary War surprise attack and the broader struggle against imperial power align with a fiercely political rock episode about resistance, anger, and fighting back.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1945, Soviet and Bulgarian troops captured Vienna after weeks of fierce fighting, ending Nazi control of the city. Beneath the rubble, its opera houses, cafés, and concert halls would slowly revive, turning a battlefield back into one of Europe’s great cultural capitals.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: The end of brutal urban fighting and the delicate rebirth of Vienna’s cultural life pair with a cinematic, quietly hopeful episode about emerging from harsh environments into beauty and fluid motion.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1948, a convoy of doctors, nurses, and medical students en route to Hadassah Hospital on Jerusalem’s Mount Scopus was ambushed in the Sheikh Jarrah neighborhood. Seventy-nine people, including a British soldier, were killed, making the Hadassah medical convoy massacre a haunting example of how even healers and patients can be drawn into the violence of war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A tragic attack on a medical convoy is contrasted with a slow, contemplative set inviting calm and healing, offering a sonic counterweight to the brutality of conflict.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1953, CIA director Allen Dulles authorised Project MKUltra, a clandestine program that experimented with drugs, hypnosis, and psychological techniques in an attempt to manipulate minds. Many tests were run without informed consent, leaving a legacy of ethical scandals that later reshaped rules for human experimentation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A secret program probing the limits of control over the mind fits with a reflective episode about our psychological dependence on technology and how modern tools shape cognition and freedom.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1976, an explosion ripped through the Lapua Cartridge Factory in Finland, killing 40 workers in the country’s deadliest modern industrial accident. The disaster devastated the small town and spurred major changes in workplace safety standards and ammunition manufacturing regulations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden, catastrophic factory blast is mirrored by an "Electric Shock" episode, using the jolt and volatility of amplified guitars as a metaphor for industrial energy and its risks.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1976, the United States reintroduced the two-dollar bill, redesigning it as a Federal Reserve Note featuring Thomas Jefferson and a vignette of the Declaration of Independence. Intended partly as a Bicentennial novelty and cost-saving measure, the bill became a quirky, rarely seen denomination surrounded by myths and superstitions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

published.match_reason: A quirky, slightly futuristic-feeling banknote revived for a national celebration fits with a spacey disco episode that turns everyday currency (four-on-the-floor beats) into something cosmic and collectible.

published.fact_text: On 13 April 1972, the Universal Postal Union recognised the People’s Republic of China as the only legitimate Chinese representative, effectively expelling the Republic of China in Taiwan from the global postal system. Even postage became geopolitics, as addresses, stamps, and mail routes suddenly reflected a new diplomatic reality.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

published.match_reason: A global decision about who gets to send and receive letters parallels an episode about who gets through to our attention, questioning the systems that decide which messages and voices arrive.

The "Beirut bus massacre" is widely seen as the flashpoint that ignited Lebanon’s 15-year civil war.
